attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman is brought into the Emergency Department after a motor vehicle crash. She is able to recall the events and was driving across an intersection when her vehicle was hit on the driver's side by a car running the red light. The car door was pushed in and she now has severe pain in the right side of her chest and this makes breathing difficult. While with the ambulance a right sided needle thoracocentesis was performed with minimal air aspirated.

On examination her blood pressure is 134/86 mmHg and pulse 92/min. As the examination proceeds the patient's work of breathing increases with her breaths becoming increasingly shallow. She has a midline trachea with reduced breath sounds on the right side with associated bruising and a focal region of chest recession on inspiration. Bedside eFAST scan shows a pneumothorax on the right.

A decision is made to undergo right sided thoracostomy with intercostal chest drain insertion, with air and a small amount of blood drained. One hour later, despite these measures, her oxygen saturations are 83% on 15 L of oxygen via a non-rebreather mask with increasing work of breathing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most important next step in management?
options: Endotracheal intubation and positive pressure ventilation
Insert an additional intercostal chest drain into the right chest
Confirm that the chest drain is in the correct place and functioning correctly.
attributes.explanation:
Whenever you do not get the response you expect - either initially or later on - from an intervention then the first response should be to check whether that intervention is still functioning. In the case of a chest drain it can malfunction either initially or later down the track. This may be from tube malposition including insertion along the outside of the rib cage or coming out, blockage of the various ports, kinking of the tube, or failure of the underwater seal drain that the tube is attached to.

Starting at either the tube or the drain is the most methodical approach. Generally if there is swinging of fluid in the drain and/or bubbling through the underwater seal drain the chest tube is in the correct location and is patent with the pleural cavity containing fluid and/or air. If the chest drain is blocked or kinked, then that should be remedied and the patient's condition reassessed. Provided her breathing becomes easier and her oxygen saturations improve, no further immediate intervention is needed. However, should her condition continue to deteriorate despite the patency of the chest drain being checked, then further action must be taken.

This patient has a flail segment with a haemopneumothorax and is suffering from hypoxia and increased work of breathing as a result. The patient is unable to maintain adequate oxygenation at this stage despite being on maximum supplemental oxygen therapy and appropriate chest drainage.

This indicates that the patient is experiencing fatigue of the muscles involved in respiratory efforts leading to respiratory failure. Early onset of pulmonary contusions would also likely be contributing. If the correct positioning of the chest drain and its patency has been checked, then endotracheal intubation and positive pressure ventilation is required.

The pathology of flail chests is two fold - not only is there fatigue of the muscles of respiration from the effort of breathing when having paradoxical movement, there is almost always underlying lung contusion which can not participate in gas exchange until healed up to weeks later. These pulmonary contusions develop progressively over hours meaning that a patient who is initially able to tolerate the insult of a flail segment may develop respiratory failure as the contusions mature.

Commencing NIV is inappropriate in the above scenario as it is unlikely to prevent further deterioration. NIV might be used as a bridge towards more definitive therapy if intubation is not initially available.

An additional chest drain into the right chest is unlikely to be of significant benefit in the above situation given that the chest drain has been checked and found to be working. Two chest drains are sometimes required in a bronchopleural fistula where massive air leaks can occur that can cause pneumothorax even if a drain is in place.

Tracheostomy may be considered in future if long term ventilation is required - which is possible for this condition - but this would not be first line management.

Emergency thoracotomy is not indicated for the above patient but may be considered if chest drain output exceeds 1.5L within a 24 hour period. Emergency room thoracotomy is often reserved for release of cardiac tamponade, open cardiac massage, prevention of air embolism and exposure of descending thoracic aorta for cross-clamping.

Other key parts of management include an aggressive analgesic regimen as rib fractures are a very painful condition. Use of intercostal nerve blocks, which spares the need for systemic analgesia with its attendant side effects, is particularly effective. If the patient fails to improve with invasive ventilation there should be Cardio-Thoracic Surgery consultation for possible surgical fixation of the ribs to achieve chest wall stabilisation.
learningPoints: Endotracheal intubation and positive pressure ventilation is required if the interventions of needle decompression and chest drainage are insufficient to address the underlying hypoxia and respiratory failure of flail chest and pneumothorax.
In cases of persistent respiratory distress after chest drain insertion, it is crucial to confirm the correct placement and function of the chest drain before proceeding with further interventions.
references: Pettiford BL, Luketich JD, Landreneau RJ. The management of flail chest. Thorac Surg Clin. 2007 Feb;17(1):25-33. doi: 10.1016/j.thorsurg.2007.02.005. PMID: 17650694. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17650694/

Dehghan N, de Mestral C, McKee MD, Schemitsch EH, Nathens A. Flail chest injuries: a review of outcomes and treatment practices from the National Trauma Data Bank. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2014 Feb;76(2):462-8. doi: 10.1097/TA.0000000000000086. PMID: 24458051. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24458051/

Weare S, Gnugnoli DM. Emergency Room Thoracotomy. [Updated 2022 Jun 21]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560863/
